qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What is the structural disposition of the word — where does it originate?
qa_findings.answer: Goodness originates in God — this is stated explicitly in the registry description (OBS-001) and confirmed by the verse evidence. Psa 119:68 (OBS-026) models the being/doing relationship at the divine level: God is good and does good. Gen 1:31 (OBS-036) establishes goodness as the ontological baseline — creation is declared very good by its maker. Human goodness is structurally derivative: a participation in and reflection of divine goodness. Mic 6:8 (OBS-027, OBS-028) confirms this — "He has told you, O man, what is good" positions the definition of goodness as divinely revealed, not humanly generated.
qa_findings.review_note: The question is well-formed and applies cleanly to goodness. No rewording required.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What determines whether the word is extended or withheld?
qa_findings.answer: The question fits goodness imperfectly — goodness as inner character is not primarily "extended or withheld" in the grace/gift sense; it is possessed or absent, expressed or suppressed. What the verse evidence does address is: (a) the conditions under which divine goodness is experienced — Psa 34:8 names trust/refuge as the response that positions one to receive it; Rom 11:22 names "continuing in his kindness" as the condition for remaining within divine chrēstotēs; (b) the conditions under which human goodness fails — 884-006 (not-good verdict) and Rom 3:12 (universal absence in fallen humanity) name the structural conditions of failure. The question is partially answered but the frame (extended/withheld) fits grace more than goodness.
qa_findings.review_note: The "extended or withheld" framing assumes a gift-disposition word. For character/virtue words like goodness, the question is better framed as: "What conditions enable or suppress the expression of this characteristic in the human person?" Recommend catalogue update.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What are the distinct modes of operation of the word in the inner being?
qa_findings.answer:
Nine distinct modes identified from the H2896A group structure (OBS-048 — DIM-67-001 resolution), plus two Greek terms adding further modes:
(1) Doxological assertion — God's character declared good (884-001)
(2) Moral character — human inner quality of goodness (884-002)
(3) Experiential orientation — nearness to God as the highest experiential good (884-003)
(4) Comparative evaluation — goodness as the ranking operator in wisdom's better-than sayings (884-004)
(5) Covenantal word — God's promise and faithfulness as good (884-005)
(6) Moral verdict — recognising what is not good; internalised moral failure (884-006)
(7) Creative pronouncement — the Creator's evaluative declaration of creation as good (884-007)
(8) Volitional preference — what is good in the eyes of / what one chooses or yields to (884-008)
(9) Inner well-being state — the shalom-condition of gladness and well-being (884-009)
(10) Spirit-produced disposition — goodness as pneumatologically enabled inner quality (885-001, G0019)
(11) Divine generous goodwill — God's kindness as inner disposition toward humanity (886-001, G5544)
(12) Spirit-produced kindness — the human expression of chrēstotēs (886-002, G5544)
qa_findings.review_note: Question is well-formed. Works well for a word with multiple groups. No change needed.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What does the word produce in the inner being of the recipient?
qa_findings.answer:
Goodness produces several inner-being states in the recipient depending on the mode:
- Encounter with divine goodness produces trust and refuge-taking (Psa 34:8, OBS-025) and receptive learning (Psa 119:68, OBS-026).
- The experiential good of nearness to God produces a stable inner orientation — the person makes God their refuge and finds what truly satisfies (Psa 73:28, OBS-029).
- Reception of God's good covenantal word produces inner knowing in heart and soul — a verified trust (Jos 23:14, OBS-033).
- The Spirit-produced quality of goodness fills the person and is completed by God (885-001 group description; Gal 5:22, OBS-042).
- The recognition of one's own not-goodness produces self-loathing and moral revulsion (Eze 36:31, OBS-035).
qa_findings.finding_type: Effect on inner being — recipient states
qa_findings.review_note: Question applies well. The variety of producer-states across modes is worth noting — goodness produces different inner states depending on whether it is received as divine encounter, covenantal word, or Spirit-fruit.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What does the word produce in the relational position of the recipient toward another?
qa_findings.answer: Mic 6:8 (OBS-027) names the relational expression of goodness as threefold: doing justice (toward others), loving kindness (toward others), walking humbly with God (vertical relation). The good person's inner character expresses itself in right conduct toward others. Col 3:12 (OBS-046 context) names kindness as a garment the renewed person puts on — a relational disposition toward the community. The comparative wisdom group (884-004) ranks inner relational qualities (humility, patience, kindness) above external achievement, implying the good person's relational orientation is characterised by these inner qualities. The Spirit-produced goodness of 885-001 is expressed in "righteous conduct and resolve" (group description).
qa_findings.finding_type: Effect on inner being — relational expression

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What does the word reveal about the disposition of God toward the human being?
qa_findings.answer:
The divine goodness groups reveal God's disposition comprehensively:
- 884-001: God's character is fundamentally good — his steadfast love, name, and Spirit are all described as good. His goodness governs his relationship to all he has made (Psa 34:8, Psa 145:9).
- 884-005: God's disposition toward his people is expressed in faithful promise-keeping — his good word does not fail (Jos 23:14, OBS-033).
- 884-007: God's disposition at creation is one of generous evaluative affirmation — he sees what he has made and declares it very good (Gen 1:31, OBS-036).
- 886-001: God's chrēstotēs (kindness) is a generous goodwill toward humanity — it leads to repentance (Rom 2:4) and is displayed supremely in Christ (Eph 2:7). It operates in a relational field alongside severity (Rom 11:22, OBS-043).
qa_findings.review_note: Question is well-formed and highly productive for goodness. No change needed.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What does the word reveal about the basis on which God's disposition toward a person is established?
qa_findings.answer: The verse evidence addresses this obliquely rather than directly. The doxological group (884-001) presents God's goodness as intrinsic to his character — it is not established on the basis of the recipient's merit but flows from who God is (Psa 86:5: "you are good and forgiving, abounding in steadfast love to all who call upon you"). The chrēstotēs group (886-001) introduces a relational conditionality — "provided you continue in his kindness" (Rom 11:22) — but this qualifies the ongoing experience of kindness, not its originating basis. The basis question is not directly answered by any anchor verse; it is implied rather than stated. Thin evidence for a direct answer.
qa_findings.stage_b_note: Inferential. The implication is that goodness is grounded in God's character, not in the recipient's merit — but no anchor verse states this explicitly for goodness the way some grace passages do.
qa_findings.review_note: The question is framed for grace/gift words where basis is explicitly discussed. For character/attribute words like goodness, the question may need a supplementary form: "Is God's goodness presented as intrinsic to his character or as conditionally responsive to human action?" Recommend adding this variant to the catalogue.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: Where, somatically or relationally, does the word locate the reality of its operation in the giver and in the recipient?
qa_findings.answer:
- In the giver (God): Goodness is located in God's character, name, steadfast love, and Spirit (884-001 group description). It is not located in a specific somatic organ. The Spirit of God (Neh 9:20, Psa 143:10) is called "good" — goodness is attributed to the divine Spirit as the operative agent.
- In the human recipient: Jos 23:14 (OBS-033) explicitly locates the reception of covenantal goodness in the "hearts and souls" — the inner being as the site of knowing. Est 5:9 (OBS-040) locates the inner well-being state in the "heart" (tov-lev — glad of heart). The experiential good of 884-003 is located in the inner reorientation of the person (Psa 73:28).
- Relationally: Mic 6:8 locates goodness in three relational practices (justice, kindness, humility with God) — outward-relational and vertical expressions.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: Does the word describe a one-time act or an ongoing condition — and how are these distinguished?
qa_findings.answer:
Both are present and are structurally distinguishable:
- One-time evaluative acts: 884-007 (God's creation pronouncements, Gen 1:4–31) and 884-008 (individual volitional decisions — "do as seems good to you") are discrete acts.
- Ongoing condition/character: 884-001 (God's goodness as enduring character — "his steadfast love endures forever," Psa 100:5), 884-002 (human moral character as ongoing inner quality), 884-003 (the experiential good of sustained nearness to God, Psa 73:28), 885-001 and 886-002 (Spirit-produced qualities as ongoing dispositions).
- The distinction: evaluative acts name discrete moments of judgment or choice; character/disposition names the enduring inner state. The Hebrew tov serves both registers without lexical distinction — context and group determine which is operative.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What spatial or directional language is used to describe the ongoing condition the word produces?
qa_findings.answer:
Spatial language is sparse but present:
- "Near" — Psa 73:28 ("it is good to be near God") — proximity as the spatial metaphor for the experiential good.
- "Refuge" — Psa 34:8 ("the man who takes refuge in him") — shelter/enclosure as the spatial consequence of tasting divine goodness.
- "Walk" — Mic 6:8 ("walk humbly with your God"), Jer 6:16 ("walk in the good way") — directional language for moral character.
- "Way" — "the good and right way" (1Sa 12:23), "the good way" (Jer 6:16) — path/direction metaphor for moral goodness.
The directional/path language is more prominent than enclosure language for this word, consistent with its Moral Character dimension.
qa_findings.review_note: Question is valid but the "ongoing condition" frame fits some goodness groups better than others. The spatial language here is primarily associated with the moral-character and experiential groups.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: Does the word produce enabling capacity in the inner being — and if so, what kind?
qa_findings.answer:
Yes — two enabling dimensions are visible:
(1) Spirit-produced goodness (885-001) enables righteous conduct and resolve — the group description says goodness is "completed by God" and "expressed in righteous conduct." The Spirit fills the person with goodness (Rom 15:14: "full of goodness, filled with all knowledge, able to instruct one another") — goodness produces the capacity to instruct and build up others.
(2) Comparative wisdom (884-004) — Pro 16:32 names self-rule and slow-to-anger as the "better" characteristics, implying these inner qualities are enabling capacities that give power over the self greater than military conquest. Ruling one's spirit is an enabling capacity for wise action.
(3) Experiential good (884-003) — the reorientation of the inner life toward what truly satisfies (Psa 73:28) produces a stable inner capacity to declare God's works — testimony as an enabled capacity.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What character quality does the word produce in the inner being?
qa_findings.answer:
Goodness in its Spirit-produced form (885-001, 886-002) generates:
- Goodness as a character quality in itself (agathōsunē — virtue/beneficence, Rom 15:14)
- Kindness as character quality (chrēstotēs — Col 3:12: put on kindness as garment of the renewed person)
- The capacity for honest moral self-assessment — 884-006 and Eze 36:31 show that genuine engagement with goodness/not-goodness produces the character quality of moral self-awareness and honest self-evaluation
- Integrity — 884-002 includes verses where "good" names the upright person who walks with integrity (Pro 19:1; Pro 28:6)
The registry description (OBS-001) frames this derivatively: the character quality of goodness in the human is a participation in divine goodness, not an independently generated virtue.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: How does the word express itself in relation to others?
qa_findings.answer:
Multiple relational expressions visible:
- Justice toward others (Mic 6:8, OBS-027) — goodness expressed as right action in the human community
- Kindness toward others (chrēstotēs — Col 3:12; Gal 5:22) — the relational disposition of generous goodwill
- Beneficence/generosity (agathōsunē — Gal 5:22 glossed as "generosity," OBS-008) — goodness expressed as outward-facing generosity
- Instructing and building up others (Rom 15:14 — "full of goodness... able to instruct one another")
- The "good news" motif (Pro 25:25, Isa 52:7) — goodness communicated as life-giving word to others
- The not-good register: 884-006 names failure in relational conduct (partiality, violence, false scales) as expressions of not-good

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What inner condition or orientation is identified as the ground of genuine expression of the word?
qa_findings.answer:
Two grounds are visible from the verse evidence:
(1) Proximity/orientation toward God — Psa 73:28 ("it is good to be near God") names nearness to God as the inner condition from which experiential goodness flows. The reorientation of the inner life toward God is the ground.
(2) Spirit-filling — 885-001 (agathōsunē) and 886-002 (chrēstotēs) name the Spirit as the enabling ground of genuine goodness in the human person. Without this, goodness is absent (Rom 3:12 — universal-depravity negation).
(3) The fear of the Lord — Pro 15:16 (Group 884-004 anchor): "better is a little with the fear of the Lord than great treasure and trouble." Fear of the Lord is the inner orientation that makes even small goodness genuine.
These three are not independent — they converge on the same inner posture: the person oriented toward God, filled by God's Spirit, whose inner life has been reoriented toward the source of goodness.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: Is there a discernible sequence or movement in the way the word operates through the inner person?
qa_findings.answer:
Yes — two sequences are visible:
(1) Divine goodness → encounter → trust/refuge → testimony (Psa 34:8, OBS-025; Psa 73:28, OBS-029): God is good → person tastes/perceives → takes refuge → declares God's works. The sequence moves from divine attribute through inner perception to relational response.
(2) Recognition of not-good → remembering → self-loathing → repentance (Eze 36:31, OBS-035): the person remembers their not-good ways → internalises the verdict → loathes themselves → the sequence implies moral transformation. This is the negative sequence that leads to the positive.
(3) Spirit-filling → goodness as character quality → expression in conduct (885-001, OBS-042): Spirit produces goodness → goodness fills the inner being → expressed in resolve and righteous conduct.
The sequences are not uniform across all twelve modes — they are characteristic of specific groups.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What direction of movement does the word follow within and through the inner person?
qa_findings.answer:
Two primary directions:
(1) Downward/inward from divine source → into human inner being. Goodness originates in God and moves into the human person through encounter (Psa 34:8), covenantal reception (Jos 23:14 — "you know in your hearts and souls"), and Spirit-filling (Gal 5:22).
(2) Outward from the inner being → into relational and communal expression. The goodness received internally is expressed outward in justice, kindness, and instruction of others (Mic 6:8; Rom 15:14; Col 3:12).
The overall direction is descending (from God) → inward (into the human inner being) → outward (into conduct and community). This is consistent with the registry description's framing of human goodness as derivative.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What does the word identify as its originating source?
qa_findings.answer: God — consistently and without exception across all groups. The divine goodness groups (884-001, 884-005, 884-007, 886-001) name God as the originating source of goodness at the level of character, promise, creation, and disposition. The human goodness groups (884-002, 884-003, 884-009, 885-001, 886-002) all present human goodness as dependent on divine source — either revealed by God (Mic 6:8), produced by God's Spirit (Gal 5:22), or enabled by nearness to God (Psa 73:28). The not-good groups (884-006, 886-002/Rom 3:12) confirm this by negation: the absence of goodness is the condition of those apart from God.
qa_findings.review_note: Q017 and Q001 overlap significantly for this word. Q001 ("structural disposition — where does it originate") and Q017 ("originating source") produce near-identical answers. Consider merging or distinguishing them more clearly in the catalogue — Q001 could focus on the structural mechanism, Q017 on the named source.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What does the word reveal about the origin of the human capacity to seek it?
qa_findings.answer: The verse evidence implies rather than states this directly. Psa 34:8 frames the encounter with divine goodness as an invitation ("taste and see") — the human capacity to respond is assumed but not explained. The experiential good of 884-003 (Psa 73:28) shows Asaph arriving at the recognition that nearness to God is good after a period of inner struggle — the capacity to arrive at this conclusion is itself a gift of reorientation. 2Th 1:11 (885-001) speaks of God fulfilling "every resolve for good" — implying the human resolve for good is itself something God completes, suggesting the capacity originates with God's action. Thin evidence for a direct answer.
qa_findings.stage_b_note: Inferential. The evidence implies that the human capacity to seek goodness is itself God-enabled, but no anchor verse states this directly for this registry.
qa_findings.review_note: Question is valid but produces thin answers for character/virtue words. For such words, the question might be supplemented with: "Does the verse evidence suggest the human capacity for goodness is innate, cultivated, or given?" — a form that opens the full range of possible answers.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: Does the word share an etymological root with another inner-being characteristic — and if so, what is that characteristic?
qa_findings.answer:
Yes — extensively. The TOV root family (OBS-007) is distributed across three registries:
- Registry 67 (this registry): H2896A (tov, adjectival — pleasant/good) — goodness as quality
- Registry 65 (generosity): G0018 (agathos) and the agatho- compound family (doing good, to do good) — goodness as active doing/generosity
- Registry 103 (love): H2895 (tov verbal — be pleasing), H2898 (tuv — goodness, nominal) — goodness as relational pleasantness/affection
The Greek OWNER terms (G0019 agathōsunē, G5544 chrēstotēs) each have related-word families (OBS-007, OBS-010): agathōsunē shares root with agathos (G0018) and all agatho- compounds; chrēstotēs shares root with chrēstos (G5543, good/kind) and chrēsteuomai (G5541, be kind) and chrēstologia (G5542, smooth talk — the distortion form).
qa_findings.review_note: This is one of the most productive questions for goodness. No wording change needed.

qa_findings.section: Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary
qa_findings.question: What does the word produce as a natural inner response in the one who receives it?
qa_findings.answer:
Three natural responses are visible from anchor verses:
(1) Trust/refuge-taking — Psa 34:8: tasting divine goodness naturally produces the response of taking refuge. The person who perceives God is good shelters in him.
(2) Praise and thanksgiving — the doxological group (884-001) consistently pairs the declaration "the Lord is good" with thanksgiving and praise (Psa 106:1, 107:1, 118:1 — "give thanks to the Lord for he is good"). The natural inner response to encountering goodness is gratitude expressed in praise.
(3) Testimony/declaration — Psa 73:28: having found that nearness to God is the highest good, the person's response is "that I may tell of all your works." Reception of goodness produces outward declaration.
(4) Receptive learning — Psa 119:68: "you are good and do good; teach me your statutes." The response to God's goodness is the desire to learn — a posture of receptive instruction.

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What inner-being logic or orientation functions as the structural opposite of the word?
qa_findings.answer:
Three structural opposites are visible from the verse evidence:
(1) The not-good verdict (884-006) — the direct linguistic opposite. The 22 not-good verses name conduct, orientations, and deeds that are structurally contrary to what is good. These include partiality, violence, false weights, and rebellious self-direction (Eze 36:31: "your evil ways, your deeds that were not good").
(2) Universal moral absence — Rom 3:12 ("no one does good, not even one") names the fallen condition as structurally characterised by the complete absence of chrēstotēs. The structural opposite of goodness in the human person is the condition of having turned aside and become worthless.
(3) Wickedness/evil as the explicit contrast partner — multiple verses in 884-002 pair good and evil as antithetical orientations (Gen 3:5 — knowing good and evil; Psa 36:4 — setting oneself in a way that is not good and not rejecting evil; Psa 38:20 — those who render evil for good).

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What effect does the logic of merit have on the inner being's capacity to receive the word?
qa_findings.answer: The question fits goodness imperfectly — goodness is not primarily a word received on the basis of merit. However, the verse evidence does address a related dynamic: Deu 9:6 explicitly denies that the good land (and by extension God's goodness) is given because of the recipient's righteousness ("not because of your righteousness, for you are a stubborn people"). This is the closest the corpus comes to addressing merit logic. The covenantal faithfulness group (884-005) consistently presents God's good word as fulfilled because of his own faithfulness, not the people's merit. The merit logic question is thus: where merit is expected, goodness is shown to operate on a different basis — divine character rather than human achievement.
qa_findings.stage_b_note: Partially answered. The merit question is addressed implicitly but not as a direct theme.
qa_findings.review_note: This question is strongly shaped by the grace/gift paradigm. For goodness, the productive parallel is: "Does the verse evidence present divine goodness as merit-based or character-based?" — a more natural fit.

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What effect does the logic of merit have on the inner being's capacity to extend the word to others?
qa_findings.answer: The comparative wisdom group (884-004) is relevant here. Pro 19:22 — "what is desired in a man is steadfast love, and a poor man is better than a liar" — places relational integrity above economic merit. Pro 22:9 — "whoever has a bountiful eye will be blessed" — names generous outward goodness as a character quality not conditioned on the recipient's merit. Mic 6:8 names doing justice and loving kindness as requirements regardless of the other's standing. The logic of merit does not determine the extension of goodness — the good person acts from character, not from calculation of the other's deservingness.
qa_findings.stage_b_note: Inferential from group evidence rather than direct anchor verse statement.

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What is the inner-being condition of the person who has received the word but failed to recognise it?
qa_findings.answer:
Two forms of non-recognition are present:
(1) The Haman instance (Est 5:9, OBS-040): inner well-being (tov-lev — glad of heart) that is not grounded in genuine goodness — a person experiencing a form of the good (honour, pleasure) but whose inner well-being is fragile, morally ungrounded, and instantly disrupted. Non-recognition here is the condition of experiencing a surface good without perceiving its source or testing its depth.
(2) The not-good group (884-006): the person who walks in ways that are not good (Isa 65:2 — "a rebellious people who walk in a way that is not good, following their own devices") is in the condition of having departed from goodness while apparently unaware of the verdict. Jer 6:16 names this directly: the people are offered "the good way" but refuse it ("we will not walk in it").

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What inner-being experience cultivates the capacity to extend the word to others?
qa_findings.answer:
Two cultivating experiences are visible:
(1) Encounter with divine goodness — Psa 34:8 (OBS-025): tasting that the Lord is good produces the inner state that positions one for trust and relational openness. The person whose inner being has been oriented toward divine goodness (Psa 73:28) naturally overflows toward others (Rom 15:14: "full of goodness... able to instruct one another").
(2) Spirit-filling — the Spirit-produced goodness of 885-001 and 886-002 is the enabling source. Gal 5:22 presents chrēstotēs and agathōsunē as Spirit-fruit — the inner experience of Spirit-filling produces the capacity to express goodness toward others. Col 3:12 frames this as "putting on" kindness — a deliberate inner act enabled by the Spirit's prior work.

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What inner-being posture does the act of earnest appeal express?
qa_findings.answer:
The question's "earnest appeal" frame fits better with words like grace or mercy where petitionary postures are common. For goodness, the closest parallel is:
(1) The volitional-preference group (884-008) — Jer 26:14 (anchor): Jeremiah's "do with me as seems good and right to you" is an act of yielded submission — not petition but surrender. The inner posture is one of willed self-relinquishment before another's judgment.
(2) The experiential group (884-003) — Psa 73:28: "I have made the Lord God my refuge" — this is a posture of taking shelter in God's goodness, an inner act of orientation rather than petition.
The inner posture most associated with goodness-seeking is submission and orientation rather than petitionary appeal.
qa_findings.stage_b_note: Partially answered — the "earnest appeal" frame does not directly apply. Inference from adjacent postures.
qa_findings.review_note: "Earnest appeal" is too narrowly framed for words where the primary seeking posture is submission or orientation rather than petition. Recommend broadening to: "What inner-being posture characterises the act of seeking or orienting toward the word?"

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What somatic forms does the inner act of seeking the word take?
qa_findings.answer:
Somatic expressions in this registry are sparse but present:
- "Taste" — Psa 34:8: sensory metaphor for inner spiritual perception. The act of tasting divine goodness is a somatic image for an inner reception event.
- "Heart" — Est 5:9 (tov-lev — glad of heart), Psa 73:28 (implied inner orientation), Jos 23:14 ("you know in your hearts and souls"). The heart is the primary somatic site named.
- "Soul" — Jos 23:14 ("hearts and souls") and Lam 3:25 ("to the soul who seeks him") name the soul as the site of seeking and receiving goodness.
- Walking — Jer 6:16 ("walk in the good way") names bodily action as the somatic expression of moral orientation.
Somatic language is present but not the primary register for goodness — the word operates more through character and orientation than through bodily expression.

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What inner-being orientation closes off the reception of the word?
qa_findings.answer:
Three closing orientations are named:
(1) Self-direction — Isa 65:2: "a rebellious people who walk in a way that is not good, following their own devices." Following one's own devices is the inner orientation that closes off the good way.
(2) Refusal of the good way — Jer 6:16: explicitly named. The people are offered "the good way" and refuse it ("we will not walk in it"). The will's refusal is the closing mechanism.
(3) Moral self-justification in wrong orientation — Jer 44:17 (884-006 notes): the people recall false prosperity in idolatry as "good" — the inner orientation that redefines what is good according to its own advantage closes off recognition of genuine goodness.
(4) Unbelief / departure — Rom 11:22 (OBS-043): "otherwise you too will be cut off" — departure from faith/trust is named as the condition that closes off the ongoing experience of divine kindness.

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What relational orientation toward others diminishes the operation of the word?
qa_findings.answer:
The not-good group (884-006) provides the primary evidence — naming specific relational failures as not-good:
- Partiality (Pro 18:5, 24:23, 28:21) — treating persons unequally in judgment diminishes goodness.
- Violence and enticing others to wrong (Pro 16:29) — the relational orientation of a violent person who leads neighbours astray.
- False weights and measures (Pro 20:23) — dishonesty in economic relations.
- Self-glorification (Pro 25:27) — seeking one's own glory at others' expense.
The common thread: the relational orientation that diminishes goodness is one of self-advantage at others' cost — partiality, dishonesty, violence. Mic 6:8 confirms the positive counterpart: justice and kindness are the relational orientations that sustain goodness.

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What is the relationship between the word and conditions of weakness in the inner being?
qa_findings.answer:
The verse evidence addresses this in two ways:
(1) Divine goodness toward the weak — Psa 34:8 ("taste and see that the Lord is good; blessed is the man who takes refuge in him") implies the one who takes refuge is in need of shelter — goodness is the resource of the vulnerable. Psa 69:16 ("your steadfast love is good; deliver me") locates the appeal to divine goodness in a context of distress. Lam 3:25 ("the Lord is good to those who wait for him, to the soul who seeks him") names waiting and seeking as postures of the weak.
(2) Pro 12:25 (884-003 non-anchor): "a good word makes him glad" — the good word as relief for inner anxiety. Goodness as word/speech directly addresses inner weakness (anxiety).
Goodness is not hostile to weakness — it operates especially in contexts of need, distress, and waiting. This is consistent with the registry description's claim that goodness is "genuinely, structurally beneficial."

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What kind of inner-being transformation does the word produce — does it change the condition or the person's orientation to the condition?
qa_findings.answer:
Both are present and distinguishable:
(1) Orientation change — the primary transformation in the experiential group (884-003) is reorientation: Psa 73:28 shows Asaph's inner life transformed not by changed external circumstances but by a reorientation toward God. "It is good to be near God" — the transformation is in where the person now locates goodness.
(2) Character transformation — the Spirit-produced goodness (885-001, 886-002) is a transformation of the person's inner character. The Spirit fills the person with goodness; Col 3:12 images this as putting on a new garment — the character itself changes.
(3) Moral awareness transformation — Eze 36:31 (OBS-035): the transformation from moral blindness to moral self-recognition is an inner condition change — the person who did not see their deeds as not-good comes to see and loathe them. This is a change of condition (from blindness to sight) that precedes orientation change (loathing → turning).

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What is the first inner-being response to receiving the word unexpectedly?
qa_findings.answer: The "unexpected reception" frame does not apply to goodness as a characteristic word. Goodness is not typically received as an unexpected gift but is either possessed (as character quality), encountered (as divine attribute), or produced (by Spirit). The closest instance is Est 5:9 where Haman receives honour unexpectedly and experiences inner gladness — but this is a morally compromised instance and does not represent genuine goodness received. No further evidence.
qa_findings.review_note: "Receiving unexpectedly" is grace-specific language. For character/virtue words, this question is generally not applicable. Could be reframed as: "What is the inner-being response when the word is first received or recognised as operative?" — which is applicable to goodness.

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What is the sequence of inner-being states as the word takes hold in the person?
qa_findings.answer:
Per Q&A-015 (OBS on sequences), two primary sequences are confirmed:
(1) Perception of divine goodness sequence: encounter → recognition ("taste and see") → trust/refuge → testimony/praise. This is the positive reception sequence (Psa 34:8, Psa 73:28).
(2) Moral conviction sequence: remembering → recognising deeds as not-good → self-loathing → (implied) repentance/turning. This is the negative-to-positive transformation sequence (Eze 36:31). The Spirit-fruit sequence: Spirit fills → goodness as quality → expression in conduct and resolve (Gal 5:22, 2Th 1:11).

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: In the eschatological context, what is the first inner-being response to the full outpouring of the word?
qa_findings.answer: The eschatological register is present but thin in this registry. Eze 36:31 is the most eschatologically charged verse — set in a new-covenant context (Ezekiel 36 is the vision of restoration) where Israel will remember, loathe, and be transformed. The inner response to the eschatological outpouring of goodness in that context is self-loathing followed by transformation (implied by the broader Ezekiel 36 context). Jer 33:11 (doxological group) situates the restoration with the refrain "the Lord is good, his steadfast love endures forever" — the first inner response to restoration being praise and thanksgiving. Thin evidence for a fuller eschatological account.
qa_findings.stage_b_note: Thin evidence.

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What is the relationship between mourning and the full reception of the word in the inner being?
qa_findings.answer: The mourning/goodness connection is not a primary theme of this registry but is present in 884-003. Lam 3:25–27 (group 884-003) names waiting, seeking, and bearing the yoke as conditions of goodness — these are postures of patient endurance that neighbour mourning. The broader Lamentations 3 context is one of profound grief and desolation in which the poet arrives at the declaration that it is good to wait for God. Mourning is thus not opposed to goodness — it is one of the inner conditions through which the recognition that "the Lord is good to those who wait" is reached. The relationship is sequential: mourning → waiting → recognition of goodness. Thin evidence for a direct statement.
qa_findings.stage_b_note: Inferential from context.
qa_findings.review_note: This question presupposes a mourning/reception sequence that is prominent in grace vocabulary. For goodness, mourning and goodness are related but not in a direct reception sequence. The question could be broadened: "What relationship does the verse evidence show between inner distress and the experience of the word?"

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What quality of inner-being stability does the word produce?
qa_findings.answer:
Goodness produces a particular quality of inner stability characterised by rootedness in God rather than in circumstances:
- Psa 73:28 (OBS-029): Asaph's inner stability after his crisis of doubt is entirely grounded in nearness to God — "it is good to be near God; I have made the Lord God my refuge." The stability is relational and divine-oriented, not circumstantial.
- The doxological group (884-001): the declaration "for he is good, his steadfast love endures forever" (Psa 100:5, 106:1, 107:1) names an enduring quality — God's goodness as the stable reference point regardless of circumstances. The human person's inner stability is anchored in this enduring divine character.
- Contrasted with Haman (Est 5:9, OBS-040): the inner well-being not grounded in God is instantly disrupted — its instability marks the absence of genuine rootedness.
The quality of stability produced by goodness is: oriented toward God, grounded in his enduring character, resistant to circumstantial disruption.

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What orientation does the stability produced by the word carry?
qa_findings.answer:
The stability produced by goodness carries a forward-and-outward orientation:
- Forward: Psa 73:28 — the inner stability of nearness to God produces testimony ("that I may tell of all your works"). The stable inner life looks outward and forward in declaration.
- Upward: Psa 119:68 — the response to God's goodness is "teach me your statutes" — a posture of ongoing receptive learning, oriented toward God.
- Outward: Rom 15:14 — "full of goodness... able to instruct one another" — the stability of a goodness-filled inner life produces capacity for community-building.
The stability is not self-enclosed or passive — it is oriented toward God (upward), toward others (outward), and toward declaration (forward).

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What capacity toward others does the word produce in the inner being of the one who has received it?
qa_findings.answer:
Three capacities are named:
(1) Instruction — Rom 15:14: "full of goodness, filled with all knowledge, and able to instruct one another." Goodness produces the capacity to teach and build up others in the community.
(2) Kindness as active relational quality — Col 3:12: chrēstotēs as garment of the renewed person enables generous relational disposition toward others. The capacity is dispositional — not occasional kindness but a settled orientation.
(3) Justice and right action — Mic 6:8: the person who knows what is good has the capacity to do justice toward others and to love kindness in relational practice.
(4) Beneficence/generosity — agathōsunē glossed as "generosity" in Gal 5:22 (OBS-008): the capacity to act generously toward others as an expression of Spirit-produced goodness.

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What is the inner-being source of the capacity to extend the word to others — is it discipline or transformation?
qa_findings.answer:
Transformation — consistently. The verse evidence does not present goodness as a disciplined achievement but as a quality that originates outside the person and transforms the inner being:
- 885-001 (agathōsunē): Spirit-produced — the Spirit fills the person with goodness. The capacity is given, not earned.
- 886-002 (chrēstotēs): Spirit-fruit — listed in Gal 5:22 as fruit (not discipline). Col 3:12 frames it as "putting on" — a response to prior transformation ("God's chosen ones, holy and beloved").
- The not-good verdict (884-006 and Rom 3:12) confirms this: the natural human condition without transformation is characterised by absence of goodness. The capacity must therefore come from outside.
- However: Mic 6:8 presents goodness in terms of what God requires and what the human person is to do — suggesting the transformed person still has an active role in expression. Transformation enables; the will enacts.
The source is transformation. Discipline may shape the expression but does not generate the capacity.

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: How does the word relate to genuine human effort — does it displace it, supplement it, or reconstitute it?
qa_findings.answer:
Reconstitutes — the clearest answer from the evidence. 2Th 1:11 (885-001): "may God fulfil every resolve for good and every work of faith by his power" — the human resolve (effort) is not displaced but completed/fulfilled by God's power. The human resolve is real; the divine completion is also real. This is reconstitution — the human will and effort are not bypassed but taken up and brought to completion.
Mic 6:8 requires human action (do justice, love kindness, walk humbly) — the requirements are genuine and demanding. The Spirit-produced character (Gal 5:22) enables rather than replaces the human acting. Pro 16:32 (comparative wisdom) presents ruling one's spirit as a human achievement — the inner discipline of self-mastery — but the group's wisdom frame implies this is a God-ordered value, not a human-autonomous one.

qa_findings.section: Section 2 — Word Impact Description
qa_findings.question: What does the word reveal about the inner-being condition of the person who does not receive it?
qa_findings.answer:
The most direct evidence: Rom 3:12 (OBS-046) — "all have turned aside; together they have become worthless; no one does good, not even one." The inner-being condition of the person without goodness is: turned aside (direction reversed), worthless (character emptied), unable to do good (capacity absent). This is the universal-depravity diagnosis — total inner-being failure at the goodness level.
Eze 36:31 (OBS-035) describes the prior condition of those who have not yet received the transforming recognition: walking in evil ways, doing deeds that are not good, under iniquity and abomination — the condition is morally blind, self-directed, unaware of the verdict on their ways.
Jer 6:16 names the condition of those who refuse the good way as one of deliberate refusal: the way is offered and rejected. This is not ignorance but wilful resistance.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does the word's primary verse reveal about its essential character?

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does the primary verse identify as the relationship between the human version and the divine version of the word?
qa_findings.answer: Mic 6:8 implies the relationship without stating it explicitly: God tells the human what is good — the knowledge of goodness flows from God to the human. The registry description (OBS-001) makes the relationship explicit: human goodness is derivative — a participation in and reflection of divine goodness. Psa 119:68 is more direct — "You are good and do good; teach me your statutes" — the human learns goodness from the God who is good. The relationship is: divine goodness is originative and instructive; human goodness is responsive and dependent.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does the primary verse identify as the primary subject of the word — who exercises it?
qa_findings.answer: Across anchor verses, both God and the human person exercise goodness, in different modes. Mic 6:8 (primary definitional anchor) — the human person is the subject required to do, love, and walk. Psa 34:8 and Psa 119:68 — God is the primary subject whose goodness is encountered and emulated. Gen 1:31 — God alone is the subject of the creation pronouncement. Gal 5:22 — the Spirit is the producing subject; the human person is the recipient-agent. The primary subject varies by group: divine goodness groups (God as subject); human character groups (human as subject); Spirit-fruit groups (Spirit as source, human as expression).

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: Does the primary verse name more than one function or dimension of the word — and if so, what is the relationship between them?
qa_findings.answer:
Mic 6:8 names three simultaneous functions of human goodness: justice (active-relational toward others), loving kindness (dispositional-relational), and humble walking with God (vertical-directional). These are co-presented as constitutive — not three optional expressions but three necessary facets of "what is good." They are in a relationship of mutual implication: genuine goodness requires all three; any one without the others is incomplete.
Psa 119:68 names two divine functions: being good (ontological) and doing good (active) — placed in causal relationship: God's being generates his doing.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does the primary verse reveal about the breadth of those toward whom the word operates?
qa_findings.answer: Psa 145:9 (doxological group 884-001): "The Lord is good to all, and his mercy is over all that he has made" — universal breadth of divine goodness toward all creation. Mic 6:8: addressed to "O man" — anthropological universality; the requirement of goodness applies to any human person before God. Gen 1:31: goodness pronounced over all creation without restriction. The breadth of goodness is universal at the divine level; at the human level, the requirement is equally universal — not restricted to a particular community.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does the verse evidence reveal about the direction of the word's operation?
qa_findings.answer: Per Q&A-016 (OBS-016 equivalent). Three directions: (1) Divine → human: God's goodness flows toward the human person through character, word, creation, and Spirit. (2) Inward → outward: goodness received in the inner being expresses itself outward in conduct, community, and testimony. (3) Vertical → horizontal: the vertical orientation (walking with God, nearness to God) produces horizontal expression (justice, kindness toward others). The directional structure is: downward from God → inward to the human person → outward to community.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does the verse evidence reveal about the word's relationship to suffering or affliction?
qa_findings.answer: Two connections are present: (1) Lam 3:25–27 (OBS in Q&A-035): in the context of profound suffering (Lamentations), the declaration that "the Lord is good to those who wait for him" situates divine goodness as sustaining and accessible in affliction. Suffering does not negate goodness — it becomes the context in which the goodness of waiting for God is discovered. (2) Psa 34:8 (OBS-025): Psalm 34 is composed in the context of David's distress. The invitation to "taste and see that the Lord is good" is issued from within a crisis. Goodness and affliction are not opposed — affliction is a context in which divine goodness is specifically offered.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does the verse evidence reveal about the word's relationship to moral failure or guilt?
qa_findings.answer: This is one of the richest connections in the registry. Eze 36:31 (OBS-035) is the primary evidence: the recognition of deeds as not-good produces self-loathing before God — moral failure is the catalyst for the deepest moral awareness. Rom 3:12 (OBS-046) names the universal absence of goodness as the diagnosis of the fallen human condition. Group 884-006 (22 verses) is entirely dedicated to the not-good verdict — moral failure named and assessed. Goodness and moral failure are structurally related: goodness is understood partly through its absence in moral failure; and the recognition of moral failure is the first movement toward goodness. The shared anchor at Eze 36:31 with Registry 1 (abomination) confirms this connection.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does the verse evidence reveal about the word's relationship to covenantal relationship?
qa_findings.answer: Group 884-005 ("God's good word and promise — covenantal faithfulness") is the primary evidence. Jos 23:14 (OBS-033): God's good promises are received and known in the "hearts and souls" of the people — covenantal faithfulness is internalised. The repeated formula in Ezra and Nehemiah ("the good hand of my God was upon me" — Ezr 7:9, Neh 2:8) names divine goodness as the operative power behind covenantal provision. God's good word is inherently covenantal — it is the promise made and kept within relationship. The doxological refrain "he is good, his steadfast love endures forever" (Psa 100:5, 106:1 etc.) links goodness directly to hesed (steadfast love) — the primary covenantal disposition of God.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What reason does a key verse give for the extension of the word — in the recipient's actions, or in the character of the one who gives it?
qa_findings.answer: Consistently in the divine goodness character of the giver, not the recipient's actions. Psa 86:5: "For you, O Lord, are good and forgiving, abounding in steadfast love to all who call upon you" — the reason is God's character. Psa 145:9: "The Lord is good to all, and his mercy is over all that he has made" — the reason is God's universal character, not recipient merit. Deu 9:6 explicitly rules out the recipient's righteousness as the basis. The extension of divine goodness is grounded in God's character; the extension of human goodness (Mic 6:8) is grounded in the requirement God places on the human person, not on the other's merit.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: Where does a key verse locate the reality of the word — in a specific faculty, organ, or location of the giver?
qa_findings.answer: In the divine giver: goodness is located in God's character, name, steadfast love, and Spirit (884-001 group). Neh 9:20 and Psa 143:10 name God's Spirit as "good" — the Spirit as the living expression of divine goodness. In the human person: Jos 23:14 names the heart and soul as the site of reception. Est 5:9 names the heart (tov-lev — glad of heart) as the location of the well-being state. There is no single somatic organ consistently named — the heart is the most frequent site, but goodness operates across character, conduct, and inner orientation more broadly.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What practical outcome does a key verse show the word producing for the recipient?
qa_findings.answer: Multiple practical outcomes: (1) Rom 15:14 — "full of goodness, filled with all knowledge, and able to instruct one another" — goodness produces the practical capacity to build up the community through instruction. (2) Psa 34:8 — "blessed is the man who takes refuge in him" — goodness produces blessedness/flourishing through trust. (3) 2Th 1:11 — "fulfil every resolve for good and every work of faith" — goodness produces completed resolve and faithful action. (4) Jos 23:14 — the practical outcome of God's good promise is its complete fulfilment — "not one word has failed." Goodness is reliably productive of what it promises.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What inner state does a key verse identify as the first response to receiving the word?
qa_findings.answer: Three first-response states across anchor verses: (1) Trust/refuge (Psa 34:8 — the one who tastes divine goodness takes refuge). (2) Praise and thanksgiving (doxological group — 884-001: "give thanks to the Lord, for he is good" — the immediate response to recognising divine goodness is gratitude). (3) Desire for instruction (Psa 119:68 — "teach me your statutes" — the response to "you are good and do good" is the desire to learn). These three — trust, gratitude, receptive learning — are the first inner states produced by genuine encounter with goodness.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What comes first in a key verse — the establishment of the relationship or standing the word confers, or the commission or responsibility that follows from it?
qa_findings.answer: Relationship precedes commission — consistently. Mic 6:8: "He has told you, O man, what is good" — the declaration comes from God to the human, establishing the knowing, before the commission (do justice, love kindness, walk humbly). Gal 5:22: the Spirit produces fruit — the Spirit's prior work is the basis; the fruit expression follows. Col 3:12: "as God's chosen ones, holy and beloved" — the identity (relationship established) precedes "put on therefore compassionate hearts, kindness..." The pattern is: prior relationship → identity established → character expressed.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does a key verse identify as the concrete content of what the word gives?
qa_findings.answer: Mic 6:8 gives the most concrete content: justice in social relations, loving kindness as relational disposition, and humble walk with God. This is the content of what goodness gives to the human community. Rom 15:14: goodness gives the capacity for mutual instruction and community edification. 2Th 1:11: goodness gives completed resolve and faithful work. Jos 23:14: God's good word gives the experience of every promise kept. The concrete content of goodness is functionally specific — not a vague quality but named actions, dispositions, and capacities.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What spatial or directional language does a key verse use for the reality of the word's operation?
qa_findings.answer: Per Q&A-010. Primary spatial language: "near" (Psa 73:28 — "good to be near God"), "refuge" (Psa 34:8 — "takes refuge"), "walk" and "way" (Mic 6:8 — "walk humbly"; Jer 6:16 — "the good way"). The spatial register of goodness is primarily directional and relational — near/far (proximity to God), shelter (refuge), and path (walking). There is no architectural or temple-specific spatial language as there might be for holiness vocabulary.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: Does any verse distinguish between the means of entry into the word and the condition the word produces?
qa_findings.answer: Psa 34:8 makes the closest distinction: "taste and see that the Lord is good" (means — active perception/encounter) → "blessed is the man who takes refuge" (condition produced — blessedness through trust). The means is perception/encounter; the condition produced is shelter and blessedness. 2Th 1:11 distinguishes between God's prior working (means — "may God fulfil every resolve for good") and the outcome (condition — "every work of faith"). The distinction between means and condition is present but not the primary analytical frame for goodness.
qa_findings.stage_b_note: Partial — the distinction is present but not foregrounded by the corpus.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: Does any verse describe the present condition produced by the word as carrying a forward orientation?
qa_findings.answer: Yes — two clear instances: (1) Psa 73:28: nearness to God (present condition of goodness) produces "that I may tell of all your works" — the forward orientation of testimony and declaration. (2) 2Th 1:11: God fulfilling the resolve for good enables "every work of faith" — present goodness is oriented toward ongoing faithful action. (3) 884-004 comparative wisdom: the better-than orientation consistently points toward choosing what endures — a future-oriented wisdom that prefers inner quality over temporal advantage.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does a key verse identify as the ground of the speaker's or recipient's identity — achievement, reception, or something else?
qa_findings.answer: Reception — consistently. Col 3:12: identity is established by prior reception — "God's chosen ones, holy and beloved" — this is their standing before the commission follows. Gal 5:22: the Spirit produces the fruit; the believer receives it. The good person in 884-002 is assessed by the quality of character (Pro 12:2 — "a good man obtains favour from the Lord") — but this character is itself a participation in divine goodness (OBS-001), not autonomous achievement. The ground of identity in this registry is reception and relationship, not self-achieved virtue.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: How does a key verse handle the co-presence of genuine human effort and prior divine initiative?
qa_findings.answer: 2Th 1:11 is the clearest evidence: "may God fulfil every resolve for good and every work of faith by his power." The human resolve and work are genuine and named first; the divine fulfilment is the completing power. Both are real; neither displaces the other. Mic 6:8: God reveals what is good (divine initiative); the human person is required to do it (genuine human effort). The co-presence is resolved through the model of reconstitution (Q&A-040): human effort is taken up and completed by divine power, not bypassed.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: In what form does a key verse present the word — and what does that form enable?
qa_findings.answer: Four primary forms: (1) Doxological declaration (884-001 — "the Lord is good") — enables praise, gratitude, trust. (2) Prophetic requirement (884-002 — Mic 6:8 "what does the Lord require") — enables moral formation and self-examination. (3) Wisdom comparative (884-004 — "better is X than Y") — enables evaluative judgment and right prioritisation. (4) Spirit-fruit list (885-001, 886-002 — Gal 5:22) — enables identification of the character cluster produced by the Spirit. Each form enables a different inner response.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What effect does the word in communicative form produce in those who receive it?
qa_findings.answer: Pro 12:25 (884-003): "a good word makes him glad" — goodness communicated as word directly lifts the inner state of the hearer. Pro 25:25: "like cold water to a thirsty soul, so is good news from a far country" — good news as relief and refreshment to the inner person. Jos 23:14: Joshua's declaration of God's faithfulness produces inner knowing in heart and soul — the communicated account of goodness deepens inner conviction. Goodness in communicative form produces gladness, relief, conviction, and inner knowledge.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: Does a key verse describe the word's work as directed toward the present only, or toward a future state?
qa_findings.answer: Both are present. The present: 884-003 group (Ecc 2:24, 3:12 — "nothing better for a person than to eat and drink and find enjoyment in his toil") — goodness as present experiential engagement. The future: 884-004 (Pro 15:16 — better a little with fear of the Lord than great treasure and trouble) — the comparative wisdom orientation evaluates present choices for future orientation. 884-005 (Jos 23:14, Jer 29:10) — God's good promise is toward a future fulfilment. The word works in both registers; the covenantal and wisdom groups are more future-oriented.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: Does any verse distinguish between a performed version and a genuine version of the word?
qa_findings.answer: Yes — two clear instances: (1) Mal 2:17 (884-002): "everyone who does evil is good in the sight of the Lord" — the prophetic indictment of a distorted inner judgment that calls evil good. This is performed/distorted goodness — the inversion of genuine assessment. (2) Jer 44:17 (884-006 notes): the people claim that their idolatrous prosperity was "good" — self-justifying redefinition of goodness in terms of material wellbeing. The genuine version (Mic 6:8) requires justice, kindness, and humility; the performed/distorted version substitutes self-advantage. (3) G5542 (chrēstologia — smooth talk) as the distortion form of chrēstotēs — using kindness language without genuine character.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What inner orientation does a key verse identify as the source of the genuine quality?
qa_findings.answer: Psa 73:28: nearness to God ("it is good to be near God; I have made the Lord God my refuge") — the genuine quality of goodness flows from the inner orientation of proximity to and trust in God. Pro 15:16: the fear of the Lord — "better a little with the fear of the Lord." Fear of the Lord as inner orientation is the ground of genuine comparative evaluation. Gal 5:22: the Spirit — the Spirit as the originating source of genuine goodness in the human person. The sources converge: fear of God, nearness to God, Spirit-enabling — all point to God-orientation as the ground.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What is the sequence of somatic and verbal response in a key verse — which comes first?
qa_findings.answer: Psa 34:8: "taste and see" (somatic/perceptual) → "blessed is the man who takes refuge" (relational response). The inner somatic perception precedes the verbal declaration and relational response. Psa 73:28: inner arrival at the recognition that nearness is good → "that I may tell" (verbal testimony follows). The pattern appears to be: inner/somatic experience or recognition → verbal/relational expression. Thin evidence — only two anchor verses directly address this sequence.
qa_findings.stage_b_note: Inferential — limited evidence base.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does a recipient's response in a key verse reveal about their inner experience of receiving the word?
qa_findings.answer: Psa 34:8: the one who tastes that the Lord is good takes refuge — the inner experience of encountering divine goodness produces a turn toward shelter and trust. Jos 23:14: "you know in your hearts and souls" — the inner experience of God's fulfilled promises is one of confirmed knowing, a settled inner certainty. Psa 73:28: "for me it is good to be near God; I have made the Lord God my refuge" — the inner experience is one of orientation and chosen proximity; making God one's refuge is an inner act of resolution. The inner experience of receiving goodness is characterised by certainty, reorientation, and trust-response.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What structural condition in a key verse establishes the recipient as having no basis for the word they receive?
qa_findings.answer: Deu 9:6 is the primary evidence: "not because of your righteousness, for you are a stubborn people" — the explicit negation of any moral basis. The good land and God's goodness are given despite (not because of) the people's character. Rom 11:22 (OBS-043) presents the condition differently — the recipient has no claim on divine kindness that is independent of God's character; the conditionality named is about continuation, not origination. The absence of any basis is more explicit in the Deuteronomy context than in the NT material for this registry.
qa_findings.stage_b_note: Partial — the no-basis condition is present but not the dominant theme of this registry.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: In a key verse, does the word operate within or across existing relational, covenantal, or social boundaries?
qa_findings.answer: Both are present: (1) Within covenant: 884-005 (God's good word to Israel) and 884-001 (doxological declarations within the covenantal community) — goodness operates primarily within the covenant relationship. (2) Across boundaries: Psa 145:9 — "the Lord is good to all, and his mercy is over all that he has made" — goodness operating universally, beyond covenantal restriction. Rom 2:4 (886-001) — God's kindness operates toward those who have not yet repented, across the moral boundary. Gen 1:31 — goodness as creation pronouncement operates across all of creation, not restricted. The movement is from covenantal particularity toward universal reach.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What is the logical structure of a key argument about the word — and what is its premise and conclusion?
qa_findings.answer: Mic 6:8 has a two-part logical structure: Premise: "He has told you, O man, what is good" (God has definitively revealed the content of goodness). Conclusion: "What does the Lord require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God?" (therefore the requirement follows from the revelation). The argument moves from revelation to requirement — what God has disclosed determines what God expects. Pro 16:32 has an implicit argument: Premise: the mighty and city-conquerors represent the highest human achievement. Premise 2: the slow-to-anger and self-ruled are better than these. Conclusion: inner mastery exceeds external achievement in value. The logical structure is comparative ranking from stated premises.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What inner-being state does the argument structure in a key verse produce in the recipient?
qa_findings.answer: Mic 6:8's argument structure produces: (1) A sense of clarity — "He has told you" implies the recipient now knows what was perhaps unclear. (2) A sense of accountability — the requirement, once clearly stated, cannot be pleaded ignorant of. (3) A posture of humility — the three requirements, especially "walk humbly," orient the inner being toward God-dependence. Pro 16:32's argument produces a revaluation of inner orientation — the recipient is invited to recognise that the inner discipline of self-rule is more valuable than they may have thought. Both arguments produce an inner reorientation of values and orientation.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What is the relationship between the divine act and the human act in a key verse — illustrative or causal?
qa_findings.answer: Causal — primarily. Psa 119:68: "You are good and do good; teach me your statutes" — God's being and doing good causes/grounds the human request for instruction. 2Th 1:11: God fulfilling the resolve for good (divine act) enables every work of faith (human act) — causal enabling. Gal 5:22: Spirit produces fruit (divine act) → human character expressed (human act) — causal. The divine act does not merely illustrate the human act; it constitutes the ground and enabling condition for it. The relationship is consistently causal, not merely illustrative.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: Where does a key verse locate the inner quality from which the word's outward act flows?
qa_findings.answer: Psa 73:28 — nearness to God is the inner quality from which testimony flows. Rom 15:14 — being "full of goodness" is the inner fullness from which the capacity to instruct others flows. Gal 5:22 — Spirit-produced character is the inner quality from which outward conduct and resolve flow. Col 3:12 — being "God's chosen ones, holy and beloved" (inner identity) is the ground from which putting on kindness (outward act) flows. The inner quality in every case is prior — it is the source, not the product, of outward expression. Location: character, Spirit-filling, identity — not a specific somatic organ consistently.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does a verse about the withdrawal or absence of the word reveal about the word's role?
qa_findings.answer: Rom 3:12 (OBS-046) is the primary evidence for universal absence: "no one does good, not even one" — the complete withdrawal/absence of chrēstotēs from fallen humanity reveals that goodness is not a default human capacity but something that requires a source outside the natural human condition. Its absence defines what fallen humanity is. Eze 36:31 (OBS-035): the recognition of deeds as not-good reveals that goodness has a structural role as the evaluative standard — its absence is measured against its presence as the norm. Jer 6:16: the absence of goodness-walking produces restlessness and judgment. The absence reveals goodness as constitutive of human flourishing and covenantal standing.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does a verse about the word in extremity reveal about its depth of operation?
qa_findings.answer: Jer 26:14 (Group 884-008 anchor, OBS-038): Jeremiah's "do with me as seems good and right to you" spoken under threat of death — this is the volitional-preference idiom in its most extreme form. The depth revealed: genuine goodness-orientation involves the capacity to yield one's own life to another's judgment. The inner depth is one of radical volitional submission. Lam 3:25–27 in extremity (national devastation, personal grief): "it is good to wait quietly for the salvation of the Lord" — goodness at its deepest is the capacity to orient toward God even in the absence of all external support.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does a verse about the word in the judicial or accountability context reveal about its function?
qa_findings.answer: Group 884-006 (not-good verdict) provides the primary judicial evidence. Eze 36:31 (OBS-035): goodness functions as the standard against which conduct is judicially assessed — deeds are measured as not-good against the norm. Eze 18:18: "he did what is not good among his people, behold he shall die for his iniquity" — the not-good verdict carries judicial consequence (death). In the judicial context, goodness functions as the standard of moral evaluation — it defines what passes and what fails the test.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does a verse about the word in the supplication context reveal about what the inner person knows about itself?
qa_findings.answer: Psa 69:16: "Answer me, O Lord, for your steadfast love is good; according to your abundant mercy, turn to me" — the supplicant appeals to God's goodness as the basis for their plea. The inner person in supplication knows: (1) their own insufficiency — they are in need of divine goodness, not self-sufficient; (2) that God's goodness is the right basis for appeal — not their own merit; (3) that divine goodness is accessible — it can be appealed to. The supplication context reveals an inner person aware of its creaturely dependence and the sufficiency of divine goodness.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does a verse about the word in the worship or liturgical context reveal about the word's function in ordered relationship with God?
qa_findings.answer: The doxological group (884-001) is entirely liturgical in character. The refrain "give thanks to the Lord, for he is good, for his steadfast love endures forever" (Psa 106:1, 107:1, 118:1, 136:1) names goodness as the primary basis for liturgical praise and thanksgiving. In the worship context, goodness functions as: (1) the ground of thanksgiving — "because he is good" is the reason for giving thanks; (2) the content of declaration — the assembled community declares the character of God; (3) an enduring quality — "his steadfast love endures forever" pairs with goodness as its temporal dimension. Goodness in liturgy is both the object of declaration and the basis for the worshipping posture.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does a verse about the word at the level of covenantal promise reveal about its scope and duration?
qa_findings.answer: Jos 23:14 and Jos 23:15 (884-005): "not one word has failed of all the good things the Lord promised... all have come to pass." The scope is complete — every good promise fulfilled. Jos 21:45 confirms: "not one word of all the good promises... had failed." The duration is covenantal — God's good word spans the entire covenantal history, from promise to fulfilment. The refrain "his steadfast love endures forever" (Psa 100:5) paired with "he is good" frames goodness as temporally unbounded. The scope is universal (toward all he has made, Psa 145:9); the duration is eternal.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does a verse about the word in the context of moral collapse reveal about the word's place in the structure of the inner life?
qa_findings.answer: Eze 36:31 in its broader context (moral collapse of Israel): goodness functions as the evaluative reference point that reveals the depth of the collapse. When the people remember their "evil ways, and their deeds that were not good" — the not-goodness is measured against the standard of what is good. Moral collapse is thus not the absence of any standard but the departure from a recognised one. The word's place in the structure of the inner life: goodness is the structural norm of inner orientation — departure from it constitutes collapse, and the recognition of departure is the first movement of restoration.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does a verse about the word in the context of formation or sanctification reveal about how the word shapes the person over time?
qa_findings.answer: Col 3:12: "put on then, as God's chosen ones, holy and beloved, compassionate hearts, kindness, humility, meekness, and patience" — the formation context is explicit. Kindness (chrēstotēs) is something put on as a deliberate act of formation — not a passive reception but an active participation in one's own sanctification. Gal 5:22: Spirit-fruit is the outcome of Spirit-work over time — fruit is not instantaneous but matures. Rom 15:14 implies a prior formation process — the community at Rome is "full of goodness, filled with all knowledge" — this fullness was not immediate but developed. Formation shapes the person toward an increasing fullness of goodness.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does a verse about the word in the context of community or fellowship reveal about the word's social dimension?
qa_findings.answer: Psa 133:1 (884-003): "Behold, how good and pleasant it is when brothers dwell in unity!" — communal goodness as the experiential quality of harmonious community life. The social dimension of goodness is explicitly named: dwelling together in unity is tov. Rom 15:14: goodness as the basis for mutual instruction — the community's goodness produces reciprocal edification. Col 3:12: kindness as a garment of the new community — goodness is a community-forming virtue, not only an individual quality. Psa 133:1 is the most explicit: goodness as the quality of community well-being when relationships are right.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
qa_findings.question: What does a verse about the word in the context of eschatology or final things reveal about the word's ultimate trajectory?
qa_findings.answer: Per Q&A-034. Eze 36:31 in its new-covenant context points toward eschatological moral transformation — the future condition when Israel will remember and loathe their past not-goodness. Jer 33:11 — the restoration refrain ("the Lord is good, his steadfast love endures forever") is placed in a future-restoration oracle, framing eschatological return in terms of goodness. 2Th 1:11 — God completing the resolve for good in the present is oriented toward the eschatological completion of faithful work. Thin evidence for a full eschatological trajectory.
qa_findings.stage_b_note: Present but not fully developed in this corpus.

qa_findings.section: ## Stage 2b Q&A Log — Section 3 (Q042–Q085): Annotated Verse Evidence
